From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Chinese surname

This article is about the surname written 李. For less common surnames written "Li", see [List of surnames romanized Li](/wiki/List_of_surnames_romanized_Li "List of surnames romanized Li"). For the Korean surname, written as 李 in hanja, see [Lee (Korean surname)](/wiki/Lee_(Korean_surname) "Lee (Korean surname)").

**Li** or **Lee** ([[lì]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Mandarin "Help:IPA/Mandarin"); [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): [李](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%9D%8E "wikt:李"); [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *Lǐ*) is a common [Chinese surname](/wiki/Chinese_surname "Chinese surname"); it is the fourth name listed in the famous *[Hundred Family Surnames](/wiki/Hundred_Family_Surnames "Hundred Family Surnames").*[[2]](#cite_note-2) Li is one of the most common surnames in Asia, shared by 92.76 million people in China,[[3]](#cite_note-stats-3) and more than 100 million in Asia.[[4]](#cite_note-eastday-4) It is the [second-most common surname](/wiki/List_of_common_Chinese_surnames "List of common Chinese surnames") in [China](/wiki/China "China") as of 2018,[[3]](#cite_note-stats-3)[[5]](#cite_note-xinhua-5) the second-most common surname in [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong"), the most common surname in [Macau](/wiki/Macau "Macau") and the 5th most common surname in [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan"), where it is usually romanized as "**Lee**". The surname is pronounced as [[lej˩˧]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Cantonese "Help:IPA/Cantonese") ([Jyutping](/wiki/Jyutping "Jyutping"): *Lei5*) in [Cantonese](/wiki/Cantonese "Cantonese"), *Lí* ([poj](/wiki/Pe%CC%8Dh-%C5%8De-j%C4%AB "Pe̍h-ōe-jī")) in [Taiwanese Hokkien](/wiki/Taiwanese_Hokkien "Taiwanese Hokkien"), but is often spelled as "**Lee**" in [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong"), [Macau](/wiki/Macau "Macau"), [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan"), [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand") and many [overseas Chinese](/wiki/Overseas_Chinese "Overseas Chinese") communities. In Macau, it is also spelled as "**Lei**". In [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia") it is commonly spelled as "**Lie**".[[6]](#cite_note-6) The common [Korean surname](/wiki/Korean_name#Family_names "Korean name"), "**[Lee](/wiki/Lee_(Korean_surname) "Lee (Korean surname)")**" (also romanized as "I", "Yi", "Ri", or "Rhee"), and the [Vietnamese surname](/wiki/Vietnamese_name#Family_name "Vietnamese name"), "**[Lý](/wiki/L%C3%BD_(Vietnamese_name) "Lý (Vietnamese name)")**", are both derived from Lee and written with the same Chinese character (李).[[7]](#cite_note-zhang-7) The character also means "plum" or "plum tree".

## Demographics and distribution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Li_(surname_%E6%9D%8E)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Demographics and distribution")]

Li or Lee (李) is one of the [most common surnames in Asia](/wiki/Lists_of_most_common_surnames_in_Asian_countries "Lists of most common surnames in Asian countries"), shared by more than 93 million people in China,[[5]](#cite_note-xinhua-5) or about 7.4% of the Chinese population.[[4]](#cite_note-eastday-4) In Asia, more than 100 million people bear the surname.[[4]](#cite_note-eastday-4) It was formerly thought to be the most common surname in China, but a 2013 analysis of the names of 1.33 billion Chinese citizens has concluded that Li is the second-most common surname, behind Wang with 95 million people.[[5]](#cite_note-xinhua-5) In 2019 it was again the second-most common surname in mainland China.[[8]](#cite_note-新京报_-_好新闻，无止境-8) Li (李) is the most common surname for the [Hakka people](/wiki/Hakka_people "Hakka people").[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Geographically, Li is one of the [most common surnames](/wiki/List_of_common_Chinese_surnames "List of common Chinese surnames") in [North China](/wiki/North_China "North China") and [Southwest China](/wiki/Southwest_China "Southwest China").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In 2019, Li was the most common surname in [Chongqing](/wiki/Chongqing "Chongqing"), [Hubei](/wiki/Hubei "Hubei"), [Hunan](/wiki/Hunan "Hunan"), [Sichuan](/wiki/Sichuan "Sichuan"), and [Yunnan](/wiki/Yunnan "Yunnan").[[8]](#cite_note-新京报_-_好新闻，无止境-8) In provinces such as [Hebei](/wiki/Hebei "Hebei"), [Heilongjiang](/wiki/Heilongjiang "Heilongjiang"), [Henan](/wiki/Henan "Henan"), [Jilin](/wiki/Jilin "Jilin"), [Shandong](/wiki/Shandong "Shandong"), [Shanxi](/wiki/Shanxi "Shanxi"), and [Yunnan](/wiki/Yunnan "Yunnan"), more than 8.8% of the local population are surnamed Li. Among all Chinese provinces, [Henan](/wiki/Henan "Henan") has the largest number of people with the surname Li, accounting for 10.3% of the total population.[[4]](#cite_note-eastday-4) Li is less common in southern and south-eastern China. Comparatively speaking, in [Fujian](/wiki/Fujian "Fujian"), [Hainan](/wiki/Hainan "Hainan"), [Jiangxi](/wiki/Jiangxi "Jiangxi"), and [Zhejiang](/wiki/Zhejiang "Zhejiang"), as well as [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan"), only 2.2 to 6.6% of the population bear this surname.[[4]](#cite_note-eastday-4)

## Origins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Li_(surname_%E6%9D%8E)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Origins")]

According to tradition, the Li surname originated from the title *Dali* held by [Gao Yao](/wiki/Gao_Yao_(minister) "Gao Yao (minister)"), a legendary minister of the [Xia dynasty](/wiki/Xia_dynasty "Xia dynasty"), and was originally written with the different character (理). There is a claim that [Laozi](/wiki/Laozi "Laozi"), the founder of [Daoism](/wiki/Daoism "Daoism"), was Li Er. Li Er is the first historical person known to have the surname Li and is regarded as the founding ancestor of the surname.

### Gao Yao

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Li_(surname_%E6%9D%8E)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Gao Yao")]

According to the ninth-century Tang dynasty text *[Yuanhe Xing Zuan](/wiki/Yuanhe_Xing_Zuan "Yuanhe Xing Zuan")*, Li is a branch of the ancient ancestral name [Ying (嬴)](/wiki/Y%C3%ADng_(surname) "Yíng (surname)") and descends from Emperor [Zhuanxu](/wiki/Zhuanxu "Zhuanxu"), grandson of the mythical [Yellow Emperor](/wiki/Yellow_Emperor "Yellow Emperor").[[10]](#cite_note-10) During the reign of [Emperor Yao](/wiki/Emperor_Yao "Emperor Yao"), [Gao Yao](/wiki/Gao_Yao_(minister) "Gao Yao (minister)") served as *Dali* (大理), or Minister of Law. [Gao Yao](/wiki/Gao_Yao_(minister) "Gao Yao (minister)")'s father was Shaohao (少昊). During the [Xia dynasty](/wiki/Xia_dynasty "Xia dynasty"), Gao Yao's descendants adopted Li (理) as their surname, from the title *Dali* (meaning "great judge").[[4]](#cite_note-eastday-4)[[7]](#cite_note-zhang-7)

It is uncertain how the character for Li changed from the original 理 to the current 李. According to popular folklore, at the end of the [Shang dynasty](/wiki/Shang_dynasty "Shang dynasty"), the minister Li Zheng (理征) was executed by [Di Xin](/wiki/Di_Xin "Di Xin") who was known for his cruelty. Li Zheng's son Li Lizhen (理利貞) escaped with his mother to the ruins of Yihou (伊侯之墟), where they survived by eating plums. In gratitude, Li Lizhen changed his surname to 李, a character that means "plum" and is a [homophone](/wiki/Homophone "Homophone") of 理. Li Lizhen was said to have settled at Ku County (苦縣), in modern [Luyi County](/wiki/Luyi_County "Luyi County"), [Henan](/wiki/Henan "Henan") province, which is regarded as the original hometown of the Li surname.[[4]](#cite_note-eastday-4)[[7]](#cite_note-zhang-7)

### Laozi

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Li_(surname_%E6%9D%8E)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Laozi")]

The first historical person known to have the surname is Li Er (李耳), better known as [Laozi](/wiki/Laozi "Laozi") (fl. 6th century BC), the philosopher who founded [Taoism](/wiki/Taoism "Taoism"). He was said to have been an eleventh-generation descendant of Li Lizhen. Laozi is widely revered as the founding ancestor of the Li surname.[[4]](#cite_note-eastday-4)[[7]](#cite_note-zhang-7)

### Ba people

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Li_(surname_%E6%9D%8E)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Ba people")]

Another early origin of Li is the non-[Huaxia](/wiki/Huaxia "Huaxia") Ba people, who established the [Ba state](/wiki/Ba_(state) "Ba (state)") during the [Zhou dynasty](/wiki/Zhou_dynasty "Zhou dynasty") in modern western [Hubei](/wiki/Hubei "Hubei") province and [Chongqing](/wiki/Chongqing "Chongqing") municipality. In 316 BC, Ba was conquered by the [state of Qin](/wiki/Qin_(state) "Qin (state)"), which would eventually conquer all the [warring states](/wiki/Seven_Warring_States "Seven Warring States") to establish the [Qin dynasty](/wiki/Qin_dynasty "Qin dynasty"). Many Ba people adopted Li as their surname, as it sounded similar to the Ba word for [tiger](/wiki/Tiger "Tiger"), which was a [totem](/wiki/Totem "Totem") for the Ba.[[4]](#cite_note-eastday-4) In 304 AD, the Ba leader [Li Xiong](/wiki/Li_Xiong "Li Xiong") (Emperor Wu) established [Cheng Han](/wiki/Cheng_Han "Cheng Han"), the first Li-surnamed dynasty in history.[[4]](#cite_note-eastday-4)

## Tang dynasty

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Li_(surname_%E6%9D%8E)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Tang dynasty")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/TangGaozu.jpg/250px-TangGaozu.jpg)](/wiki/File:TangGaozu.jpg)

Li Yuan (Emperor Gaozu), founder of the Tang dynasty

See also: [List of emperors of the Tang dynasty](/wiki/List_of_emperors_of_the_Tang_dynasty "List of emperors of the Tang dynasty")

As the surname of the emperors of the [Tang dynasty](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty"), Li was bestowed upon or adopted by numerous people. During the period, it became one of the most common [Chinese surnames](/wiki/Chinese_surname "Chinese surname").

Li was the imperial surname of the [Tang dynasty](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty"), founded by [Li Yuan](/wiki/Emperor_Gaozu_of_Tang "Emperor Gaozu of Tang") in 618 AD. One of the most prosperous and influential dynasties in Chinese history, Tang was ruled by 20 emperors surnamed Li.[[4]](#cite_note-eastday-4)[[7]](#cite_note-zhang-7) The Tang dynasty imperial family belonged to the northwest military aristocracy prevalent during the [Sui dynasty](/wiki/Sui_dynasty "Sui dynasty") and claimed to be [paternally descended](/wiki/Patrilineality "Patrilineality") from [Laozi](/wiki/Laozi "Laozi"),[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-Woolf2007-12)[[13]](#cite_note-Hargett2006-13) the [Qin](/wiki/Qin_(state) "Qin (state)") general [Li Xin](/wiki/Li_Xin_(Qin) "Li Xin (Qin)"), the [Han dynasty](/wiki/Han_dynasty "Han dynasty") general [Li Guang](/wiki/Li_Guang "Li Guang"), and [Western Liang](/wiki/Western_Liang_(Sixteen_Kingdoms) "Western Liang (Sixteen Kingdoms)") ruler [Li Gao](/wiki/Li_Gao "Li Gao").[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)

The Tang emperors liberally granted the royal surname to favoured generals, officials, and their clans, such as [Xu Shiji](/wiki/Xu_Shiji "Xu Shiji"), [Du Fuwei](/wiki/Du_Fuwei "Du Fuwei"), and [Guo Zihe](/wiki/Guo_Zihe "Guo Zihe") (郭子和). Many non-Han people under Tang's rule were also granted the Li surname, including the [Eastern Tujue](/wiki/Eastern_Tujue "Eastern Tujue") khan [Ashina Simo](/wiki/Ashina_Simo "Ashina Simo") (Li Simo), [Mohe](/wiki/Mohe_people "Mohe people") leaders [Li Duozuo](/wiki/Li_Duozuo "Li Duozuo") and [Li Jinxing](/w/index.php?title=Li_Jinxing&action=edit&redlink=1 "Li Jinxing (page does not exist)") (李謹行), [Khitan](/wiki/Khitan_people "Khitan people") leaders [Li Guangbi](/wiki/Li_Guangbi "Li Guangbi") and [Li Jinzhong](/wiki/Li_Jinzhong "Li Jinzhong"), and [Goguryo](/wiki/Goguryo "Goguryo") general [Li Zhengji](/wiki/Li_Zhengji "Li Zhengji"). Some [Tibetans](/wiki/Tibetans "Tibetans"), [Uyghurs](/wiki/Uyghurs "Uyghurs"), [Persians](/wiki/Persian_people "Persian people"), and [Jews](/wiki/Jews "Jews") were also granted the Li surname. The number of people surnamed Li skyrocketed during the Tang dynasty.[[4]](#cite_note-eastday-4)[[7]](#cite_note-zhang-7)

## Other dynasties ruled by Li families

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Li_(surname_%E6%9D%8E)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Other dynasties ruled by Li families")]

During the [Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period](/wiki/Five_Dynasties_and_Ten_Kingdoms_period "Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period") following the fall of Tang in 907, the [Shatuo](/wiki/Shatuo "Shatuo") general [Li Keyong](/wiki/Li_Keyong "Li Keyong"), who had been granted the Li surname, established the [Jin State](/wiki/Jin_(Later_Tang_precursor) "Jin (Later Tang precursor)"), precursor of the [Later Tang](/wiki/Later_Tang "Later Tang"). [Li Bian](/wiki/Li_Bian "Li Bian") (Xu Zhigao), the founder of the [Southern Tang](/wiki/Southern_Tang "Southern Tang"), also changed his surname to Li. The emperors of Later Tang and Southern Tang continued the Tang tradition of liberally bestowing the Li surname on their favoured people.[[7]](#cite_note-zhang-7)

Other Li-surnamed dynasties include [Liang](/wiki/Li_Gui_(warlord) "Li Gui (warlord)"), [Western Xia](/wiki/Western_Xia "Western Xia"), and [Shun](/wiki/Shun_dynasty "Shun dynasty"). All told, there have been 64 Li-surnamed emperors in Chinese history, ruling all or part of China for 650 years.[[7]](#cite_note-zhang-7)

## Adoption by non-Han Chinese peoples

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Li_(surname_%E6%9D%8E)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Adoption by non-Han Chinese peoples")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/T%C6%B0%E1%BB%A3ng_L%C3%BD_Th%C3%A1i_T%E1%BB%95.jpg/250px-T%C6%B0%E1%BB%A3ng_L%C3%BD_Th%C3%A1i_T%E1%BB%95.jpg)](/wiki/File:T%C6%B0%E1%BB%A3ng_L%C3%BD_Th%C3%A1i_T%E1%BB%95.jpg)

Lý Thái Tổ, founder of the Vietnamese Lý dynasty

Influenced by [Chinese culture](/wiki/Chinese_culture "Chinese culture"), many non-[Han Chinese](/wiki/Han_Chinese "Han Chinese") peoples living in and near China have adopted Chinese-style surnames throughout history, and Li is one of the most common surnames adopted. Li has been used by the [Bai people](/wiki/Bai_people "Bai people") for more than 1,000 years, and is one of the top three surnames among the Bai. Li is the fifth of the twelve most common surnames of the [Yao people](/wiki/Yao_people_(Asia) "Yao people (Asia)"), who adopted the name more than 800 years ago. Li also has history of 500 years among the [Miao people](/wiki/Miao_people "Miao people"). Among the 55 [officially recognized ethnic minorities](/wiki/List_of_ethnic_groups_in_China "List of ethnic groups in China") in China, 33 are known to use the Li surname.[[7]](#cite_note-zhang-7)

Outside China, the surname has also been adopted in Korea and Vietnam.

The first recording of the Korean Lee, Yi (이) surname appeared as early as in the early [Three Kingdoms of Korea period](/wiki/Three_Kingdoms_of_Korea "Three Kingdoms of Korea") (57 BCE – 668 CE) and was adopted among noblemen. Today, Lee (romanized as Lee, I, Yi (South Korea), Ri (North Korea)) is one of the top five Korean surnames. The surname today traces its roots to two main families in Korea. The first, the most famous, is the [Jeonju Yi clan](/wiki/Jeonju_Yi_clan "Jeonju Yi clan"), the surname of [Yi Seong-gye](/wiki/Yi_Seong-gye "Yi Seong-gye"), 이성계, the first ruler of the [Joseon dynasty](/wiki/Joseon_dynasty "Joseon dynasty"). Yi was also the last ruling surname in Korea and ruled for around 500 years. The second is the Gyeongju Lee clan, which was founded by Yi Al-pyeong, 이알평), one of the [village headmen](/wiki/Village_head "Village head") who chose [Park Hyeokgeose](/wiki/Hyeokgeose_of_Silla "Hyeokgeose of Silla") as the first king of [Silla](/wiki/Silla "Silla"). According to the *[Samguk Sagi](/wiki/Samguk_sagi "Samguk sagi")*, the Yi name was officially bestowed on the family by [King Yuri of Silla](/wiki/Yuri_of_Silla "Yuri of Silla") around 9 BCE.

Li (spelled [Lý](/wiki/L%C3%BD_(Vietnamese_name) "Lý (Vietnamese name)") in modern Vietnamese) has been used in [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam") for more than 2,000 years since the [Han dynasty](/wiki/Han_dynasty "Han dynasty"), when [Northern Vietnam](/wiki/Northern_Vietnam "Northern Vietnam") was ruled as a province of the Han Empire. In 544, Vietnam gained temporary independence from China when [Lý Nam Đế](/wiki/L%C3%BD_Nam_%C4%90%E1%BA%BF "Lý Nam Đế") founded the [Early Lý dynasty](/wiki/Early_L%C3%BD_dynasty "Early Lý dynasty"). Lý Nam Đế (Li Nan Di), as an ethnic Chinese, ruled as an emperor of Vietnam. In 1009, [Lý Thái Tổ](/wiki/L%C3%BD_Th%C3%A1i_T%E1%BB%95 "Lý Thái Tổ") established the Later [Lý dynasty](/wiki/L%C3%BD_dynasty "Lý dynasty"), which ruled Vietnam for more than 200 years.[[7]](#cite_note-zhang-7) In 1232, after the Lý dynasty was replaced by the [Trần dynasty](/wiki/Tr%E1%BA%A7n_dynasty "Trần dynasty"), [Grand Preceptor](/wiki/Grand_Preceptor "Grand Preceptor") [Trần Thủ Độ](/wiki/Tr%E1%BA%A7n_Th%E1%BB%A7_%C4%90%E1%BB%99 "Trần Thủ Độ") made some descendants of the Lý family change their surname to [Nguyễn](/wiki/Nguy%E1%BB%85n "Nguyễn").[[16]](#cite_note-nvk-16)

## Prominent clans

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Li_(surname_%E6%9D%8E)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Prominent clans")]

There are historically twelve prominent clans (*junwang*, 郡望) of Li, the most famous being those of the [Longxi](/wiki/Longxi_Commandery "Longxi Commandery") and [Zhao](/wiki/Zhao_Commandery "Zhao Commandery") [commanderies](/wiki/Commandery_(China) "Commandery (China)"). The Zhao clan, based in modern [Hebei](/wiki/Hebei "Hebei")'s [Zhao County](/wiki/Zhao_County "Zhao County"), traces its origin from [Li Mu](/wiki/Li_Mu "Li Mu") (died 229 BC), Lord Wu'an of the [State of Zhao](/wiki/State_of_Zhao "State of Zhao"), a general of the [Warring States period](/wiki/Warring_States_period "Warring States period"). The Zhao clan produced 17 [prime ministers](/wiki/Chinese_chancellor "Chinese chancellor") during the Tang dynasty.[[17]](#cite_note-stnn-17) In 2010, a group of nine large tombs of the Zhao clan were discovered in [Zanhuang County](/wiki/Zanhuang_County "Zanhuang County"), dating from the [Northern Dynasties](/wiki/Northern_Dynasties "Northern Dynasties").[[18]](#cite_note-18)

The Zhao clan remained the most prominent branch of the Li until it was surpassed by the Longxi during the Tang dynasty. The Longxi clan is named after the Longxi Commandery in southern [Gansu](/wiki/Gansu "Gansu") province. Li Chong (李崇), the first Qin governor of Longxi, is revered as its founder. The Han general [Li Guang](/wiki/Li_Guang "Li Guang"), famous for defeating the [Xiongnu](/wiki/Xiongnu "Xiongnu"), came from Longxi. Centuries later, the Tang emperors traced their ancestry to the Longxi clan, making it the most prominent branch ever since.[[17]](#cite_note-stnn-17) The Longxi Li is considered one of the four great cultural traditions of Gansu province. [Longxi County](/wiki/Longxi_County "Longxi County") has built a museum for the Li clan, and hosted the Longxi Li cultural festival in 2012.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

There are 2,157 [genealogy books](/wiki/Genealogy_book "Genealogy book") of Li families known to be extant.[[4]](#cite_note-eastday-4)

## Historical distribution and migration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Li_(surname_%E6%9D%8E)&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Historical distribution and migration")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Kingmenlee.jpg/250px-Kingmenlee.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kingmenlee.jpg)

Lee Family Temple, [Kinmen](/wiki/Kinmen "Kinmen"), Taiwan

Having originated in what is now Henan province, Li spread to [Shanxi](/wiki/Shanxi "Shanxi"), Hebei, [Shaanxi](/wiki/Shaanxi "Shaanxi"), [Sichuan](/wiki/Sichuan "Sichuan"), and [Hubei](/wiki/Hubei "Hubei") provinces by the end of the [Warring States period](/wiki/Warring_States_period "Warring States period"). During the Qin dynasty, the Li clan spread to [Guangdong](/wiki/Guangdong "Guangdong") and [Guangxi](/wiki/Guangxi "Guangxi") provinces, while Li Chong established the Longxi Li clan in Gansu.[[4]](#cite_note-eastday-4)

During the early Tang dynasty many Lis migrated to [Fujian](/wiki/Fujian "Fujian") and [Hainan](/wiki/Hainan "Hainan") provinces. After the mass adoption of the imperial Li surname during the Tang period, Li became the second-most common surname during the subsequent Song dynasty, after Wang. There were approximately 5.6 million people with the surname, or 7.2% of the total population at the time, with large concentrations in the northern provinces of Hebei, Henan, and [Shandong](/wiki/Shandong "Shandong").[[4]](#cite_note-eastday-4)

The [Mongol invasion of China](/wiki/Mongol_invasion_of_China "Mongol invasion of China") in the 13th century caused widespread depopulation in northern China. Li, being over-represented in the north, was hit especially hard. In the [Ming dynasty](/wiki/Ming_dynasty "Ming dynasty") following the Mongol [Yuan dynasty](/wiki/Yuan_dynasty "Yuan dynasty"), the Li-surnamed population had declined to 5.1 million, constituting 5.5% of the total population. It fell behind [Zhang](/wiki/Zhang_(surname) "Zhang (surname)") to become the third most common surname of the time. Many of the Lis had migrated to [South China](/wiki/South_China "South China") by this period.[[4]](#cite_note-eastday-4)

After the Chinese began immigrating to the West, a significant population of Li's now reside in the United States. Many have adopted the homophonic English surname "Lee," elevating it to the [22nd-most common](/wiki/List_of_most_common_surnames_in_North_America#United_States "List of most common surnames in North America") surname. Over 30% of people with the surname Lee in America identify as Asian/Pacific Islander in origin, making it the most common Chinese surname in the United States.[[20]](#cite_note-US-20)
